meta.title: EU Family Reunification Guide
meta.description: How to join family legally in an EU country — EU Family Reunification Directive rights, requirements, and process.

hero.title: EU Family Reunification
hero.sub: If you have a close family member who is an EU citizen or has legal residence in an EU country, you may be able to join them through the EU Family Reunification Directive. This keeps families together legally, safely, without dangerous crossings.

alert.text: Family reunification is a legal right under EU law. Even if you entered the EU irregularly, if your close family member has legal status, you can regularise your situation through this route. Always get legal advice first.

types.name: Spouse / Registered Partner
types.desc: The husband, wife, or registered partner of an EU resident. Most EU countries require at least 2 years of legal residency by the sponsor before applying.

types.name: Dependent Children
types.desc: Unmarried children under 18 of the sponsor or their spouse. Children over 12 may face additional integration requirements in some countries.

types.name: Dependent Parents
types.desc: Parents who are dependent on the sponsor. Requirements vary significantly by country — typically requires proof of dependency and that the parent cannot be supported in their home country.

types.name: Refugee Family Reunion (Faster)
types.desc: If the sponsor is a recognised refugee, the process is faster and requirements are reduced. Some EU countries waive income requirements for refugee family reunion.

requirements: The EU sponsor has legal and stable residence (usually a permit of at least 1 year and reasonable prospect of permanent residence).
The sponsor has adequate housing — not overcrowded.
The sponsor has sufficient income to support the family (varies by country — typically above social assistance level).
Health insurance covering the family member applying.
No threat to public policy or public security.

steps.title: Check the rules in the specific EU country
steps.desc: Rules vary significantly by country. Germany, France, Netherlands, and Sweden all have different income thresholds, waiting periods, and language requirements.

steps.title: The EU sponsor applies in their country of residence
steps.desc: The family member already in the EU applies to the local immigration authority on behalf of the family member abroad.

steps.title: The applicant applies at the embassy
steps.desc: Once approved in principle, the family member abroad applies for a family reunion visa at the relevant EU embassy in their home country.

steps.title: Integration requirements
steps.desc: Many EU countries require a basic language test before entry (Germany, Netherlands, France). Some offer waivers for refugees, elderly, or those with severe disability.

steps.title: Arrive and settle
steps.desc: After approval, you enter with a temporary residence permit. After several years, you can apply for a permanent residence permit and eventually citizenship.
